failure_surface: Task evaluation returned 0 (failed). Agent cleared all columns A-F instead of only A-C, lost column F data, and performed row deletion instead of selective cell clearing as instructed.
root_cause: The agent misunderstood the scope of the operation, applying bulk row replacement instead of selective cell clearing. Key errors: (1) code wrapped in <execute_code> tags causing SyntaxError delays, (2) clear loop covered all 7 columns instead of 3, (3) column F was overwritten with None despite explicit instruction to leave it untouched, (4) filtered_rows only wrote 5 columns losing column F data permanently.
minimal_fix_hypothesis: Before executing write/clear operations, explicitly verify each column index against the instruction's specified column ranges, ensuring operations only affect the exact columns mentioned and leave other columns completely unmodified.
evidence: Instruction states 'Clear ONLY the data in columns A, B, and C' but code executes: for c in range(1, 7): ws.cell(row=r, column=c).value = None - clearing columns A-F instead
Instruction states 'without touching column F' but column F data is overwritten with None during the clear phase
Filtered rows are written back using: for c in range(1, 6): covering only columns A-E, permanently losing any column F values
Agent attempted code execution multiple times with <execute_code> wrapper causing SyntaxError before succeeding on last attempt
Evaluation test_case_results: [0] indicates complete failure despite successful file save

memory_items.title: Selective Column Operations Require Explicit Range Verification
memory_items.lesson: When clearing or writing cells, always verify the column index range matches the instruction exactly. Clearing 'columns A-C' means iterating range(1, 4), not range(1, 7).
memory_items.generalization: Cross-check every loop or operation's column bounds against the stated scope before execution to prevent unintended data loss in adjacent columns.

memory_items.title: Column Preservation Requires Targeted Read/Write Patterns
memory_items.lesson: If an instruction specifies leaving certain columns untouched, ensure both the read phase and write phase exclude those columns from modification operations.
memory_items.generalization: Separate data extraction and restoration into distinct phases: identify protected columns first, then structure read/write loops to preserve them.

memory_items.title: Validate Code Execution Environment Before Submission
memory_items.lesson: Avoid wrapping Python code in XML-like tags (e.g., <execute_code>) when direct execution is expected, as this causes SyntaxError failures.
memory_items.generalization: Ensure submitted code follows the exact format requirements of the execution environment without additional markup.

edits.content:
## Trace-Derived Lessons

### Lesson 1: Verify Operation Scope Against Instructions
When modifying specific columns, explicitly count and verify that iteration bounds match the instruction. For example, "columns A-C" translates to `range(1, 4)` not `range(1, 7)`. Always cross-reference column indices with the natural language specification before executing bulk operations.

### Lesson 2: Preserve Protected Columns During Transformations
When instructions specify leaving certain columns untouched (e.g., "without touching column F"), implement protective measures:
- Exclude protected columns from clear/delete operations
- Include protected column values when writing restored data
- Validate that both read and write phases respect the protection boundaries

### Lesson 3: Execute Code Without Environment-Incompatible Wrappers
Avoid wrapping executable code in XML-style tags when the execution environment expects raw Python. Format compliance prevents SyntaxError failures that delay task completion.
